question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2025

Code: 65/4/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 38 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This Question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Question paper is divided into FIVE Sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A - Question Number 1 to 18 are Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) and Question Number {tex}19 \& 20{/tex} are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B - Question Number 21 to 25 are Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C - Question Number 26 to 31 are Short Answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D - Question Number 32 to 35 are Long Answer (LA) type questions, carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E - Question Number 36 to 38 are case study based questions, carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section - B, 3 questions in Section - C, 2 questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}{2}{/tex} questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.
  9. Use of calculator is NOT allowed.

Section - A

This section comprises multiple choice questions (MCQs) of 1 mark each.
1.

Domain of {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(2 x^{2}-3\right){/tex} is:

a. {tex}(-1,0) \cup(1, \sqrt{2}){/tex}
b. {tex}(-\sqrt{2},-1) \cup(0,1){/tex}
c. {tex}[-\sqrt{2},-1] \cup[1, \sqrt{2}]{/tex}
d. {tex}(-\sqrt{2},-1) \cup(1, \sqrt{2}){/tex}
1
2.

The matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}0 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 0 & -7 \\ 2 & 7 & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex} is a:

a. diagonal matrix
b. symmetric matrix
c. skew symmetric matrix
d. scalar matrix
1
3.

If {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}3 x-2, & 0 \lt x \leq 1 \\ 2 x^{2}+a x, & 1 \lt x \lt 2\end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous for {tex}x \in(0,2){/tex}, then a is equal to:

a. -4
b. {tex}-\frac{7}{2}{/tex}
c. -2
d. -1
1
4.

If {tex}y=\log _{2 x}(\sqrt{2 x}){/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to:

a. 0
b. 1
c. {tex}\frac{1}{{x}}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 {x}}}{/tex}
1
5.

If {tex}{f}: {N} \rightarrow {W}{/tex} is defined as {tex}{f}({n})= \begin{cases}\frac{{n}}{2}, & \text { if } {n} \text { is even } \\ 0, & \text { if } {n} \text { is odd }\end{cases}{/tex} then f is:

a. injective only
b. surjective only
c. a bijection
d. neither surjective nor injective
1
6.

The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}{A}(-2,3,5){/tex} on the y -axis is:

a. {tex}(0,0,5){/tex}
b. {tex}(0,3,0){/tex}
c. {tex}(-2,0,5){/tex}
d. {tex}(-2,0,0){/tex}
1
7.

If A and B are invertible matrices of order {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} such that {tex}\operatorname{det}({A})=4{/tex} and {tex}\operatorname{det}\left[({AB})^{-1}\right]=\frac{1}{20}{/tex}, then {tex}\operatorname{det}({B}){/tex} is equal to:

a. {tex}\frac{1}{20}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
c. 20
d. 5
1
8.

For real {tex}x{/tex}, let {tex}f(x)=x^{3}+5 x+1{/tex}. Then:

a. {tex}f{/tex} is one-one but not onto on {tex}R{/tex}
b. {tex}f{/tex} is onto on {tex}R{/tex} but not one-one
c. {tex}f{/tex} is one-one and onto on {tex}R{/tex}
d. {tex}f{/tex} is neither one-one nor onto on {tex}R{/tex}
1
9.

The values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} so that {tex}f(x)=\sin x-\cos x-\lambda x+C{/tex} decreases for all real values of {tex}x{/tex} are:

a. {tex}1 \lt \lambda \lt \sqrt{2}{/tex}
b. {tex}\lambda \geq 1{/tex}
c. {tex}\lambda \geq \sqrt{2}{/tex}
d. {tex}\lambda \lt 1{/tex}
1
10.

If {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} are square matrices of same order such that {tex}A B=A{/tex} and {tex}{BA}={B}{/tex}, then {tex}{A}^{2}+{B}^{2}{/tex} is equal to:

a. {tex}{A}+{B}{/tex}
b. BA
c. {tex}2({~A}+{B}){/tex}
d. 2 BA
1
11.

The area of the region enclosed by the curve {tex}y=\sqrt{x}{/tex} and the lines {tex}x=0{/tex} and {tex}{x}=4{/tex} and x -axis is:

a. {tex}\frac{16}{9}{/tex} sq. units
b. {tex}\frac{32}{9}{/tex} sq. units
c. {tex}\frac{16}{3}{/tex} sq. units
d. {tex}\frac{32}{3}{/tex} sq. units
1
12.

The value of {tex}\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d x}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
c. {tex}\tan ^{-1} e-\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
d. {tex}\tan ^{-1} {e}{/tex}
1
13.

The corner points of the feasible region of a Linear Programming Problem are {tex}(0,2),(3,0),(6,0),(6,8){/tex} and {tex}(0,5){/tex}. If {tex}{Z}={ax}+{by}{/tex}; ({tex}{a}, {b} \gt 0{/tex}) be the objective function, and maximum value of Z is obtained at (0, 2) and {tex}(3,0){/tex}, then the relation between a and b is:

a. {tex}{a}={b}{/tex}
b. {tex}{a}=3 {~b}{/tex}
c. {tex}{b}=6 {a}{/tex}
d. {tex}3 {a}=2 {~b}{/tex}
1
14.

If {tex}\int e^{-3 \log x} d x=f(x)+C{/tex}, then {tex}f(x){/tex} is:

a. {tex}{e}^{-3 \log {x}}{/tex}
b. {tex}{e}^{\log \left(\frac{1}{{x}^{3}}\right)}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{-1}{2 {x}^{2}}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{-1}{4 x^{4}}{/tex}
1
15.

The function {tex}f{/tex} defined by {tex}{f}({x})= \begin{cases}{x}, & \text { if } {x} \leq 1 \\ 5, & \text { if } {x} \gt 1\end{cases}{/tex} is not continuous at:

a. {tex}{x}=0{/tex}
b. {tex}{x}=1{/tex}
c. {tex}x=2{/tex}
d. {tex}x=5{/tex}
1
16.

The solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-x}{y}{/tex} represents family of:

a. parabolas
b. circles
c. ellipses
d. hyperbolas
1
17.

If the sides {tex}A B{/tex} and {tex}A C{/tex} of {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} are represented by vectors {tex}\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively, then the length of the median through {tex}A{/tex} on BC is:

a. {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} units
b. {tex}\sqrt{18}{/tex} units
c. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{34}}{2}{/tex} units
d. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{48}}{2}{/tex} units
1
18.

If {tex}f(x)=2 x+\cos x{/tex}, then {tex}f(x){/tex}:

a. has a maxima at {tex}x=\pi{/tex}
b. has a minima at {tex}{x}=\pi{/tex}
c. is an increasing function
d. is a decreasing function
1

Questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion and Reason based questions. Two statements are given, one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
19.

Assertion (A): If A and B are two events such that {tex}{P}({A} \cap {B})=0{/tex}, then A and B are independent events.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: Two events are independent if the occurrence of one does not effect the occurrence of the other.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
20.

Assertion (A): In a Linear Programming Problem, if the feasible region is empty, then the Linear Programming Problem has no solution.
Reason (R): A feasible region is defined as the region that satisfies all the constraints.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1

Section - B

This section comprises very short answer (VSA) type questions of {tex}{2}{/tex} marks each.
21.

If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rr}2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2\end{array}\right]{/tex} and {tex}A^{2}=k A{/tex}, then find the value of {tex}k{/tex}.

 2
22.(i)

Simplify {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{{x}}{\sqrt{1+{x}^{2}}}\right){/tex}.

 2

OR
(ii)

Find domain of {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{{x}-1}{/tex}.

 2
23.

Calculate the area of the region bounded by the curve {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} and the x -axis using integration.

 2
24.

Find the least value of 'a' so that {tex}{f}({x})=2 {x}^{2}-{ax}+3{/tex} is an increasing function on {tex}[2,4]{/tex}.

 1

OR
(ii)

If {tex}f(x)=x+\frac{1}{x}, x \geq 1{/tex}, show that {tex}f{/tex} is an increasing function.

 2
25.

A cylindrical water container has developed a leak at the bottom. The water is leaking at the rate of {tex}5 {cm}^{3} / {s}{/tex} from the leak. If the radius of the container is 15 cm , find the rate at which the height of water is decreasing inside the container, when the height of water is 2 metres.

 2

Section - C

This section comprises short answer (SA) type questions of {tex}{3}{/tex} marks each.
26.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}} d x{/tex}

 3
27.

Find the distance of the point {tex}(-1,-5,-10){/tex} from the point of intersection of the lines {tex}\frac{{x}-1}{2}=\frac{{y}-2}{3}=\frac{{z}-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{5}=\frac{{y}-1}{2}={z}{/tex}.

 2
28.(i)

If {tex}f: R^{+} \rightarrow R{/tex} is defined as {tex}f(x)=\log _{a} x(a \gt 0{/tex} and {tex}a \neq 1){/tex}, prove that {tex}f{/tex} is a bijection.
({tex}R^{+}{/tex}is a set of all positive real numbers.)

 3

OR
(ii)

Let {tex}{A}=\{1,2,3\}{/tex} and {tex}{B}=\{4,5,6\}{/tex}. A relation R from A to B is defined as {tex}R=\{(x, y): x+y=6, x \in A, y \in B\}{/tex}.

  1. Write all elements of {tex}R{/tex}.
  2. Is R a function? Justify.
  3. Determine domain and range of {tex}R{/tex}.
 3
29.(i)

The probability distribution of a random variable X is given by:

X 0 1 2 3
{tex}{P}({X}){/tex} p {tex}\frac{{p}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{p}}{6}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{p}}{12}{/tex}
  1. Determine the value of p.
  2. Calculate {tex}{P}({X} \geq 1){/tex}.
  3. Calculate expectation of X, i.e. {tex}{E}({X}){/tex}
 1

OR
(ii)

In a city, a survey was conducted among residents about their preferred mode of commuting. It was found that {tex}50 \%{/tex} people preferred using public transport, 35\% preferred using a bicycle and {tex}20 \%{/tex} use both public transport and a bicycle. If a person is selected at random, find the probability that:

  1. The person uses only public transport.
  2. The person uses a bicycle, given that they also use the public transport.
  3. The person uses neither public transport nor a bicycle.
 3
30.(i)

Find k so that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x^{2}-2 x-3}{x+1}, & x \neq-1 \\ k, & x=-1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at {tex}{x}=-1{/tex}.

 3

OR
(ii)

Check the differentiability of function {tex}{f}({x})={x}|{x}|{/tex} at {tex}{x}=0{/tex}.

 3
31.


For the given graph of a Linear Programming Problem, write all the constraints satisfying the given feasible region.

 3

Section - D

This section comprises long answer (LA) type questions of 5 marks each.
32.

The relation between the height of the plant ({tex}y {~cm}{/tex}) with respect to exposure to sunlight is governed by the equation {tex}{y}=4 {x}-\frac{1}{2} {x}^{2}{/tex}, where x is the number of days exposed to sunlight.

  1. Find the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight. (2)
  2. In how many days will the plant attain its maximum height? What is the maximum height? (3)
 5
33.(i)

Show that area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}. Also find the area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}.

 5

OR
(ii)

Find the equation of a line in vector and cartesian form which passes through the point ({tex}1,2,-4{/tex}) and is perpendicular to the lines {tex}\frac{{x}-8}{3}=\frac{{y}+19}{-16}=\frac{{z}-10}{7}{/tex}, and {tex}\vec{r}=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) .{/tex}

 5
34.(i)

Evaluate {tex} \int _ { 0 } ^ { 3 / 2 } | x \cos \pi x | d x{/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

Find: {tex}\int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x }{/tex}

 5
35.

If A is a {tex}3 \times 3{/tex} invertible matrix, show that for any scalar {tex}{k} \neq 0{/tex}, {tex}(k A)^{-1}=\frac{1}{k} A^{-1}{/tex}. Hence calculate {tex}(3 A)^{-1}{/tex}, where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

 5

Section - E

This section comprises 3 case study based questions of 4 marks each.

Case Study - 1
36.

Some students are having a misconception while comparing decimals. For example, a student may mention that {tex}78.56 \gt 78.9{/tex} as {tex}7856 \gt 789{/tex}. In order to assess this concept, a decimal comparison test was administered to the students of class VI through the following question : In the recently held Sports Day in the school, 5 students participated in a javelin throw competition. The distances to which they have thrown the javelin are shown below in the table :

Name of student Distance of javelin (in meters)
Ajay {tex}47 .7{/tex}
Bijoy {tex}47 .07{/tex}
Kartik {tex}43.09{/tex}
Dinesh {tex}43 . 9{/tex}
Devesh {tex}45.2{/tex}

The students were asked to identify who has thrown the javelin the farthest.
Based on the test attempted by the students, the teacher concludes that {tex}40 \%{/tex} of the students have the misconception in the concept of decimal comparison and the rest do not have the misconception. {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students having misconception answered Bijoy as the correct answer in the paper. {tex}90 \%{/tex} of the students who are identified with not having misconception, did not answer Bijoy as their answer.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. What is the probability of a student not having misconception but still answers Bijoy in the test? (1)
  2. What is the probability that a randomly selected student answers Bijoy as his answer in the test? (1)
    1. What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is having misconception? (2)

      OR

    2.  What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is amongst students who do not have the misconception? (2)
 4

Case Study - 2
37.

An engineer is designing a new metro rail network in a city.

Initially, two metro lines, Line A and Line B, each consisting of multiple stations are designed. The track for Line A is represented by {tex}l_{1}: \frac{{x}-2}{3}=\frac{{y}+1}{-2}=\frac{{z}-3}{4}{/tex}, while the track for Line B is represented by {tex}l_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{2}=\frac{{y}-3}{1}=\frac{{z}+2}{-3}{/tex}.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find whether the two metro tracks are parallel. (1)
  2. Solar panels are to be installed on the rooftop of the metro stations. Determine the equation of the line representing the placement of solar panels on the rooftop of Line A's stations, given that panels are to be positioned parallel to Line A's track ({tex}l_{1}{/tex}) and pass through the point {tex}(1,-2,-3){/tex}. (1)
    1. To connect the stations, a pedestrian pathway perpendicular to the two metro lines is to be constructed which passes through point ( {tex}3,2,1{/tex} ). Determine the equation of the pedestrian walkway. (2)

      OR

    2. Find the shortest distance between Line A and Line B. (2)
 4

Case Study - 3
28.

During a heavy gaming session, the temperature of a student's laptop processor increases significantly. After the session, the processor begins to cool down, and the rate of cooling is proportional to the difference between the processor's temperature and the room temperature ({tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex}). Initially the processor's temperature is {tex}85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. The rate of cooling is defined by the equation {tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({T}({t}))=-{k}({T}({t})-25){/tex},
where {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} represents the temperature of the processor at time t (in minutes) and k is a constant.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the expression for temperature of processor, {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} given that {tex}{T}(0)=85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. (2)
  2. How long will it take for the processor's temperature to reach {tex}40^{\circ} {C}{/tex}? Given that {tex}{k}=0 \cdot 03, \log _{{e}} 4=1 \cdot 3863{/tex}. (2)
 4

Find the least value of 'a' so that {tex}{f}({x})=2 {x}^{2}-{ax}+3{/tex} is an increasing function on {tex}[2,4]{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}f(x)=2 x^{2}-a x+3 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x-a{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Least value of {tex}a{/tex} does not exist.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}} d x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}I=\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}} d x{/tex}
Put {tex}x^{3 / 2}=t \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d t}{1+\sqrt{t}}{/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Put} \sqrt{t}=z \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{t}} d t=d z{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{2 z}{1+z} d z{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\int 1 d z-\int \frac{1}{1+z} d z\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}[z-\log |1+z|]+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}[\sqrt{t}-\log |1+\sqrt{t}|]+C{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4}{3}\left[\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}-\log \left|1+\sqrt{x^{3 / 2}}\right|\right]+C{/tex}

{tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}

{tex}2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex}
Solving, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex}
Since, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex} satisfy {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex}
Now distance of {tex}(-1,-5,-10){/tex} from {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex} is:
{tex}\sqrt{(-1+1)^{2}+(-1+5)^{2}+(-1+10)^{2}}=\sqrt{97}{/tex} units

32.

The relation between the height of the plant ({tex}y {~cm}{/tex}) with respect to exposure to sunlight is governed by the equation {tex}{y}=4 {x}-\frac{1}{2} {x}^{2}{/tex}, where x is the number of days exposed to sunlight.

  1. Find the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight. (2)
  2. In how many days will the plant attain its maximum height? What is the maximum height? (3)
Solution:

  1. Rate of Growth of the Plant: The rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is the derivative of the height function {tex}y{/tex} with respect to time {tex}x{/tex}. The height function is given by:
    {tex} y=4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2 {/tex}
    Differentiating with respect to {tex}x{/tex}, we get:
    {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex}
    Thus, the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight is:
    {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=4-x . {/tex}

  2. Maximum Height: To find when the plant reaches its maximum height, we set the rate of growth {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} to zero:
    {tex} 4-x-0 \rightarrow x-4 . {/tex}
    Thus, the plant reaches its maximum height in 4 days. Now, substitute {tex}x=4{/tex} into the height equation to find the maximum height:
    {tex} y-4(4)-\frac{1}{2}(4)^2-16-8-8 {~cm} {/tex}
    Hence, the maximum height of the plant is 8 cm.

Show that area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}. Also find the area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}.

Solution:

Let ABCD be a parallelogram such the 

{tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{p}, \vec{A D}=\vec{q} \Rightarrow \vec{B C}=\vec{q}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{A C}=\vec{A B}+\vec{B C}=\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{a}{/tex} .....(i) [By triangle law of vectors]
And {tex}\vec{B D}=\vec{B A}+\vec{A D}=-\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{b}{/tex}  .....(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\bar{a}+\bar{b}=2 \bar{q}{/tex}
Subtracting (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \vec{p}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{p}=\frac{1}{2}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{p} \times \vec{q}=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}(\bar{a} \times \bar{a}+\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\vec{b} \times \bar{a}-\bar{b} \times \bar{b}){/tex}
So, the area of a parallelogram ABCD = {tex}|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}
Now, area of a parallelogram, whose diagonals are {tex}2 i-j+k{/tex} and {tex}i+3 j-\dot{k}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|(2 i-j+k) \times(i+3 j-k)|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|i(1-3)-j(-2-1)+k(6+1)|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4+9+49}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{62} \text { sq. units }{/tex}.

Evaluate {tex} \int _ { 0 } ^ { 3 / 2 } | x \cos \pi x | d x{/tex}

Solution:

According to the question ,  {tex} I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { 3 / 2 } | x \cos \pi x | d x{/tex}
consider, x cos {tex} \pi{/tex}x = 0

So, let us divide the integral by x = {tex} \frac 12{/tex} , we get 
Now ,
{tex} | x \cos \pi x | = \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { x \cos \pi x , } & { 0 \leq x \leq \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \\ { - x \cos \pi x , } & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \leq x \leq \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
Now, {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } | x \cos \pi x | d x + \int _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 } |x \cos \pi x | d x{/tex}

Let I1 = {tex}\int_{} {\mathop x\limits_I } \mathop {\cos \pi x}\limits_{II} dx{/tex}
By Using integration by parts  , we get 
{tex}= x \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } - \int \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { x \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, From Eq.(i), we have
{tex}I =\left[ x \frac { \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 / 2 } - \left[ \frac { x \sin \pi x } { \pi } + \frac { \cos \pi x } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { 1 / 2 } ^ { 3 / 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \left( 0 + \frac { \cos 0 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \right) \right]{/tex}{tex}- \left[ \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { 3 \pi } { 2 } \right)\right.{/tex}{tex}- \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \pi ^ { 2 } } \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) ]{/tex}

Find: {tex}\int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x }{/tex}

Solution:

According to the question, {tex}I = \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + \sin 2 x } {/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { d x } { \sin x + 2 \sin x \cos x }[ \because \sin 2 x = 2 \sin x \cos x ]{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { d x } { \sin x ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) }{/tex}
Multiplying numerator and denominator by {tex}sinx{/tex}

 {tex} = \int \frac { \sin x } { \sin ^ { 2 } x ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { \sin x } { \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) ( 1 + 2 \cos x ) } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}cos x = t{/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-sin\ x\ dx = dt{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}sin x\ dx = -dt{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = \int \frac { - d t } { \left( 1 - t ^ { 2 } \right) ( 1 + 2 t ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \int \frac { - d t } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) } [\because a^2-b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)]{/tex}
Now,

using partial fractions,
let{tex}\frac { 1 } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) }= \frac { A } { 1 - t } + \frac { B } { 1 + t } + \frac { C } { 1 + 2 t }{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = (1 + t) (1 + 2t) A + (1 - t) (1 + 2t) B+ (1 - t) (1 + t) C ... (i)
Putting t = -1 in Eq. (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow1 = ( 2 ) ( - 1 ) B \Rightarrow B = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
Putting t = 1 in Eq. (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow 1 = 2 \cdot ( 3 ) A \Rightarrow A = \frac { 1 } { 6 }{/tex}
Putting {tex}t = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow1 = \left( 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \left( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad C = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \int\frac { -1 } { ( 1 - t ) ( 1 + t ) ( 1 + 2 t ) } dt= -\Bigg[\int \frac { A } { 1 - t } d t + \int \frac { B } { 1 + t } d t + \int \frac { C } { 1 + 2 t } d t\Bigg]{/tex}{tex}=-\Bigg[ \frac { 1 } { 6 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 - t } + \frac {- 1 } { 2 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + t } + \frac { 4 } { 3 } \int \frac { d t } { 1 + 2 t } \Bigg]{/tex}

Solution:

{tex} \Rightarrow k A \text { and } \frac{1}{k} A^{-1} \text { are inverse of each other. } {/tex}

Case Study - 1
36.

Some students are having a misconception while comparing decimals. For example, a student may mention that {tex}78.56 \gt 78.9{/tex} as {tex}7856 \gt 789{/tex}. In order to assess this concept, a decimal comparison test was administered to the students of class VI through the following question : In the recently held Sports Day in the school, 5 students participated in a javelin throw competition. The distances to which they have thrown the javelin are shown below in the table :

Name of student Distance of javelin (in meters)
Ajay {tex}47 .7{/tex}
Bijoy {tex}47 .07{/tex}
Kartik {tex}43.09{/tex}
Dinesh {tex}43 . 9{/tex}
Devesh {tex}45.2{/tex}

The students were asked to identify who has thrown the javelin the farthest.
Based on the test attempted by the students, the teacher concludes that {tex}40 \%{/tex} of the students have the misconception in the concept of decimal comparison and the rest do not have the misconception. {tex}80 \%{/tex} of the students having misconception answered Bijoy as the correct answer in the paper. {tex}90 \%{/tex} of the students who are identified with not having misconception, did not answer Bijoy as their answer.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. What is the probability of a student not having misconception but still answers Bijoy in the test? (1)
  2. What is the probability that a randomly selected student answers Bijoy as his answer in the test? (1)
    1. What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is having misconception? (2)

      OR

    2.  What is the probability that a student who answered as Bijoy is amongst students who do not have the misconception? (2)
Solution:

Let {tex}E_1{/tex} : Student has a misconception
{tex}E_2{/tex} : Student does not have misconception
A: Student answered Bijoy as correct
{tex} \therefore P\left(E_1\right)=\frac{40}{100}, {P}\left(E_2\right)=\frac{60}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(A \mid E_1\right)=\frac{80}{100}, P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100} {/tex}
{tex} P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_1\right)=\frac{20}{100}, P\left(\bar{A} \mid E_2\right)=\frac{90}{100} {/tex}

  1. {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=\frac{10}{100}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
  2. {tex}P(A)={P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right){/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}+\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{38}{100} \text { or } \frac{19}{50} {/tex}
    1. {tex}P\left(E_1 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)}{P(A)}{/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{\frac{40}{100} \times \frac{80}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{16}{19} {/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right)=\frac{{P}\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)}{P(A)}{/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{\frac{60}{100} \times \frac{10}{100}}{\frac{38}{100}}=\frac{3}{19} {/tex}

  2. Solar panels are to be installed on the rooftop of the metro stations.     1. To connect the stations, a pedestrian pathway perpendicular to the two metro lines is to be constructed which passes through point ( {tex}3,2,1{/tex} ). Determine the equation of the pedestrian walkway. (2)

      OR

    2. Find the shortest distance between Line A and Line B. (2)
Solution:

{tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{-2}=\frac{z-3}{4} ; {/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z+2}{-3}{/tex}

  1. Drs of {tex}l_{1}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 3,-2,4\rangle{/tex}, drs of {tex}l_{2}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 2,1,-3\rangle{/tex} as drs are not proportional, hence {tex}l_{1}{/tex} is not parallel to {tex}l_{2}{/tex}.
  2. Equations of line parallel to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and passing through {tex}(1,-2,-3){/tex} is
    {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+3}{4} \text { or } \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
    1. The pathway is perpendicular to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and {tex}l_{2} \ldots{/tex}. It is parallel to {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}{/tex}
      {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & -3\end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of pathway is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}\vec{b}_{1}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}\right|{/tex}
      {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{c}(-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) \\ \sqrt{4+289+49}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{31}{\sqrt{342}}{/tex}

Case Study - 3
28.

During a heavy gaming session, the temperature of a student's laptop processor increases significantly. After the session, the processor begins to cool down, and the rate of cooling is proportional to the difference between the processor's temperature and the room temperature ({tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex}). Initially the processor's temperature is {tex}85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. The rate of cooling is defined by the equation {tex}\frac{{d}}{{dt}}({T}({t}))=-{k}({T}({t})-25){/tex},
where {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} represents the temperature of the processor at time t (in minutes) and k is a constant.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the expression for temperature of processor, {tex}{T}({t}){/tex} given that {tex}{T}(0)=85^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. (2)
  2. How long will it take for the processor's temperature to reach {tex}40^{\circ} {C}{/tex}? Given that {tex}{k}=0 \cdot 03, \log _{{e}} 4=1 \cdot 3863{/tex}. (2)
Solution:
  1. {tex}\frac{d T}{d t}=-k(T-25){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d T}{T-25}=-k \int d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log |T-25|=-k t+C{/tex}
    When {tex}t=0, T=85{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log 60=C{/tex}
    Using in equation {tex}(a), \log |T-25|=-k t+\log 60{/tex} ...(b)
  2. When {tex}k=0.03, \log |T-25|=-0.03 t+\log 60{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left|\frac{T-25}{60}\right|=-0.03 t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow T-25=60 . e^{-0.03 t}{/tex}
    When {tex}T=40, t=t_{1}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{15}{60}=e^{-0.03 t_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow e^{-0.03 t_{1}}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow-0.03 t_{1}=-\log 4{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow t_{1}=\frac{\log 4}{0.03}=\frac{1.3863}{0.03}=46.21 {~m}{/tex}
